2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions

50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.

92

94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.

100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. 104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. 156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.

4171	\begin{lemma}[\(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) is local on a finite spanning cover]
4172	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover,
4174	    AlgebraicGeometry.exists_sum_pow_eq_one, AlgebraicGeometry.mem_range_of_span_pow,
4175	    AlgebraicGeometry.eq_zero_of_span_pow, AlgebraicGeometry.map_smul_endFun,
4176	    AlgebraicGeometry.bump_eq, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_surj, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_eq}
4177	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(R\)-modules, let
4178	  \(g : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-linear map, let \(f \in R\), and let
4179	  \(s : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to R\) be a finite family with
4180	  \(\operatorname{span}\{s_1, \ldots, s_n\} = R\) (the unit ideal). For each \(j\) write
4181	  \(M_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers} s_j)^{-1} M\) and \(N_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers}
4182	  s_j)^{-1} N\) for the localisations at the powers of \(s_j\), and let
4183	  \(g_{s_j} : M_{s_j} \to N_{s_j}\) be the induced \(R\)-linear (equivalently
4184	  \(R_{s_j}\)-linear) map. Suppose that for every \(j\) the localised map \(g_{s_j}\)
4185	  exhibits \(N_{s_j}\) as the localisation of \(M_{s_j}\) at the powers of (the image of)
4186	  \(f\), i.e.\ \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\). Then
4187	  \(g\) itself exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(f\):
4188	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4189	\end{lemma}
4190	\begin{proof}
4191	
4192	  This is a purely commutative-algebra statement: the predicate
4193	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\) is the conjunction of its
4194	  three defining conditions, and each is verified by descent along the spanning cover. Throughout we use the standard partition
4195	  of unity: since \(\operatorname{span}\{s_j\} = R\), for any chosen exponents \(N_j\) the
4196	  powers \(s_j^{N_j}\) again span the unit ideal, so there are \(r_j \in R\) with
4197	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{N_j} = 1\); and an element of an \(R\)-module is zero iff its image in
4198	  \(M_{s_j}\) vanishes for every \(j\) (locality of being zero on a spanning cover, the
4199	  \(s_j\)-torsion form of the same partition of unity).
4200	
4201	  \emph{\(f\) acts invertibly on \(N\).} For each \(j\), the hypothesis
4202	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) makes multiplication
4203	  by \(f\) on \(N_{s_j}\) bijective. Multiplication by \(f\) on \(N\) is an isomorphism iff it
4204	  is so after localising at each \(s_j\) (an \(R\)-linear endomorphism is bijective iff it is
4205	  bijective on the cover \(\{D(s_j)\}\), since injectivity and surjectivity are both local on
4206	  a spanning cover); hence \(f \cdot (-) : N \to N\) is bijective, and likewise every power
4207	  \(f^k\).
4208	
4209	  \emph{Every \(y \in N\) satisfies \(f^k y = g(x)\) for some \(x \in M\), \(k\).}
4210	  Fix \(y \in N\). For each \(j\), surjectivity of \(g_{s_j}\) as a localisation at the powers
4211	  of \(f\) yields \(m_j \in M\), exponents \(a_j\) (clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator) and
4212	  \(k_j\) with \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) holding after localising at \(s_j\); clearing
4213	  the remaining \(s_j\)-torsion (enlarging \(a_j\)) we may take this as an honest equality
4214	  \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) in \(N\). Choose a common \(k = \max_j k_j\) (replacing
4215	  \(m_j\) by \(f^{k - k_j} m_j\)) so that \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = g(m_j)\) for all \(j\). Pick,
4216	  by the partition of unity, \(r_j\) with \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} = 1\) (possible because the
4217	  \(s_j^{a_j}\) span the unit ideal). Then
4218	  \(f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j\, g(m_j) = g\bigl(\sum_j r_j m_j\bigr)\),
4219	  so \(x = \sum_j r_j m_j\) and the exponent \(k\) work.
4220	
4221	  \emph{If \(g(x) = g(x')\) then \(f^k(x - x') = 0\) for some \(k\).} Put
4222	  \(z = x - x'\), so \(g(z) = 0\). For each \(j\), the equaliser clause of
4223	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) gives, from the
4224	  vanishing of \(g_{s_j}(z) = 0\) in \(N_{s_j}\), an exponent \(k_j\) with
4225	  \(f^{k_j} z = 0\) after localising at \(s_j\); clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator there is
4226	  \(b_j\) with \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k_j} z = 0\) in \(M\). Take \(k = \max_j k_j\), so
4227	  \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\) for all \(j\). With \(r_j\) chosen so that
4228	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} = 1\),
4229	  \(f^{k} z = \sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\). Hence \(f^{k}(x - x') = 0\).
4230	
4231	  The three clauses are exactly the data of
4232	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4233	\end{proof}

4398	  \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}})\) (using that the stalk functor, being a
4399	  filtered colimit, is itself exact and commutes with forming the kernel). Being a
4400	  stalkwise isomorphism, the comparison map is an isomorphism of sheaves, so
4401	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\).
4402	  The same stalkwise-flatness argument applied to a finite diagram of \(R\)-modules shows
4403	  that \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits to finite limits; equivalently
4404	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left exact, i.e.\ preserves finite limits (kernels).

4409	      additive.} The stalk of \(\widetilde{M}\) at \(x\) is computed as a stalk of the
4410	      underlying sheaf of abelian groups, so the induced stalk map is \emph{a priori}
4411	      only a map of abelian groups. To know it is the localised \(R\)-module map
4412	      \(M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}}\) — and hence injective/exact by the
4413	      flatness of localisation — one identifies it on germs with the linear localisation
4414	      map: the germ \(\operatorname{germ}_x\) is \(R_{\mathfrak{p}}\)-linear (the linear
4415	      germ \(\operatorname{germ}_\ell\)), and the stalk action of the abelian-group stalk
4416	      functor on \(\widetilde{\varphi}\) agrees with \(\varphi\) on the image of the
4417	      structure map \(\widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}_x\). This germ-naturality identity
4418	      is the load-bearing step.

4421	      % `StructureSheaf.germₗ` and the Γ-linearity of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app`; this packages
4422	      % the abelian-group stalk map `σ_x` as the R-linear localisation map and identifies it
4423	      % with `IsLocalizedModule.map …`, whose injectivity is the already-built
4424	      % `tilde_toStalk_map_injective`.
4425	    \item \textbf{(B) Stalkwise isomorphism \(\Rightarrow\) isomorphism of sheaves.} A
4426	      morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is an isomorphism on every
4427	      stalk is an isomorphism; equivalently the family of stalk functors jointly reflects
4428	      isomorphisms (and jointly reflects the finite limits being compared). One checks the
4429	      kernel-comparison map is a stalk-wise isomorphism by (A), then concludes it is an

4440	      sections-level localisation flatness from (A)–(B) applies directly. This reduction
4441	      lets the proof work entirely at the abelian-presheaf/stalk level.

/-- Evaluation on an object `X` gives a functor

def evaluation (X : Cᵒᵖ) : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ ModuleCat.{v} (R.obj.obj X) :=

/-- The forget functor `SheafOfModules R ⥤ Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`. -/
@[simps]
noncomputable def toSheaf : SheafOfModules.{v} R ⥤ Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v} where
  obj M := ⟨_, M.isSheaf⟩
  map f := { hom := (forget R ⋙ PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R.obj).map f }

/--
The forgetful functor from sheaves of modules over sheaf of ring `R` to sheaves of `R(X)`-module
when `X` is initial.
-/
@[simps]
noncomputable def forgetToSheafModuleCat
      (X : Cᵒᵖ) (hX : Limits.IsInitial X) :
    SheafOfModules.{w} R ⥤ Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{w} (R.1.obj X)) where
  obj M := ⟨(PresheafOfModules.forgetToPresheafModuleCat X hX).obj M.1,
    Presheaf.isSheaf_of_isSheaf_comp _ _

  map f := { hom := (PresheafOfModules.forgetToPresheafModuleCat X hX).map f.1 }

1241	\begin{lemma}
1242	\label{lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1243	Let
1244	$(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
1245	$(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1246	be affine schemes.
1247	Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
1248	morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
1249	$\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
1250	\begin{enumerate}
1251	\item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
1252	functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
1253	\item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
1254	in the $S$-module $N$.
1255	\end{enumerate}
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	
1258	\begin{proof}
1259	The first assertion follows from the identification in
1260	Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
1261	and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}.
1262	The second assertion follows from the fact
1263	that $\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))$ and hence
1264	$$
1265	\psi_* \widetilde N(D(f)) = \widetilde N(D(\psi^\sharp(f))) =
1266	N_{\psi^\sharp(f)} = (N_R)_f = \widetilde{N_R}(D(f))
1267	$$
1268	as desired.
1269	\end{proof}
1270	
1271	\noindent
1272	Lemma \ref{lemma-widetilde-pullback} above says in particular
1273	that if you restrict
1274	the sheaf $\widetilde M$ to a standard affine open subspace
1275	$D(f)$, then you get $\widetilde{M_f}$. We will use this from
1276	now on without further mention.
1277	
1278	\begin{lemma}
1279	\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
1280	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
1281	be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a
1282	quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
1283	$\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to
1284	the $R$-module $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$.
1285	\end{lemma}
1286	
1287	\begin{proof}
1288	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1289	Since every standard open $D(f)$ is quasi-compact we see that
1290	$X$ is locally quasi-compact, i.e., every point has a fundamental
1291	system of quasi-compact neighbourhoods, see Topology,
1292	Definition \ref{topology-definition-locally-quasi-compact}.
1293	Hence by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-quasi-coherent-module}
1294	for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
1295	there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
1296	$\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
1297	sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
1298	In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
1299	By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
1300	covering to  a standard open covering.
1301	Thus we get a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup D(f_i)$
1302	and $R_{f_i}$-modules $M_i$ and isomorphisms
1303	$\varphi_i : \mathcal{F}|_{D(f_i)} \to \mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
1304	for some $R_{f_i}$-module $M_i$. On the overlaps
1305	we get isomorphisms
1306	$$
1307	\xymatrix{
1308	\mathcal{F}_{M_i}|_{D(f_if_j)}
1309	\ar[rr]^{\varphi_i^{-1}|_{D(f_if_j)}}
1310	& &
1311	\mathcal{F}|_{D(f_if_j)}
1312	\ar[rr]^{\varphi_j|_{D(f_if_j)}}
1313	& &
1314	\mathcal{F}_{M_j}|_{D(f_if_j)}.
1315	}
1316	$$
1317	Let us denote these $\psi_{ij}$. It is clear that
1318	we have the cocycle condition
1319	$$
1320	\psi_{jk}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1321	\circ
1322	\psi_{ij}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1323	=
1324	\psi_{ik}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
1325	$$
1326	on triple overlaps.
1327	
1328	\medskip\noindent
1329	Recall that each of the open subspaces $D(f_i)$, $D(f_if_j)$,
1330	$D(f_if_jf_k)$ is an affine scheme. Hence the sheaves $\mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
1331	are isomorphic to the sheaves $\widetilde M_i$ by Lemma
1332	\ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above. In particular we see that
1333	$\mathcal{F}_{M_i}(D(f_if_j)) = (M_i)_{f_j}$, etc.
1334	Also by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above we see
1335	that $\psi_{ij}$ corresponds to a unique $R_{f_if_j}$-module isomorphism
1336	$$
1337	\psi_{ij} : (M_i)_{f_j} \longrightarrow (M_j)_{f_i}
1338	$$
1339	namely, the effect of $\psi_{ij}$ on sections over $D(f_if_j)$.
1340	Moreover these then satisfy the cocycle condition that
1341	$$
1342	\xymatrix{
1343	(M_i)_{f_jf_k}
1344	\ar[rd]_{\psi_{ij}}
1345	\ar[rr]^{\psi_{ik}}
1346	& &
1347	(M_k)_{f_if_j} \\
1348	&
1349	(M_j)_{f_if_k} \ar[ru]_{\psi_{jk}}
1350	}
1351	$$
1352	commutes (for any triple $i, j, k$).
1353	
1354	\medskip\noindent
1355	Now Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-glue-modules}
1356	shows that there exist an $R$-module $M$ such that
1357	$M_i = M_{f_i}$ compatible with the morphisms $\psi_{ij}$.
1358	Consider $\mathcal{F}_M = \widetilde M$. At this point it is
1359	a formality to show that $\widetilde M$ is isomorphic to
1360	the quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we started out with.
1361	Namely, the sheaves $\mathcal{F}$ and $\widetilde M$ give
1362	rise to isomorphic sets of glueing data of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1363	with respect to the covering $X = \bigcup D(f_i)$, see
1364	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves}
1365	and in particular Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-mapping-property-glue}.
1366	Explicitly, in the current situation, this boils down to
1367	the following argument: Let us construct an $R$-module map
1368	$$
1369	M \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}).
1370	$$
1371	Namely, given $m \in M$ we get $m_i = m/1 \in M_{f_i} = M_i$
1372	by construction of $M$. By construction of $M_i$ this corresponds
1373	to a section $s_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$. (Namely, $\varphi^{-1}_i(m_i)$.)
1374	We claim that $s_i|_{D(f_if_j)} = s_j|_{D(f_if_j)}$. This is
1375	true because, by construction of $M$, we have $\psi_{ij}(m_i) = m_j$,
1376	and by the construction of the $\psi_{ij}$. By the sheaf condition of
1377	$\mathcal{F}$ this collection of sections gives rise to a unique
1378	section $s$ of $\mathcal{F}$ over $X$. We leave it to the reader
1379	to show that $m \mapsto s$ is a $R$-module map.
1380	By Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we obtain an associated
1381	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module map
1382	$$
1383	\widetilde M \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}.
1384	$$
1385	By construction this map reduces to the isomorphisms
1386	$\varphi_i^{-1}$ on each $D(f_i)$ and hence is an isomorphism.
1387	\end{proof}

  \mathcal{F}\) is the natural morphism of sheaves of modules whose component over a distinguished
  open \(D(f)\) is the localization lift \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.lift}\) of the
  section-restriction map \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\)
  along the structure-sheaf localization \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\) (the latter
  exhibiting \(\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde{M}) = M_f\) as a localization at the powers of \(f\)).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Criterion for the counit to be an isomorphism]
  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  For an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), the counit
  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(\mathcal{F}\) lies in the
  essential image of the tilde functor \(M \mapsto \widetilde{M}\); equivalently
  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\) for some \(R\)-module \(M\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[The forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms]
  \label{lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib}
  \lean{SheafOfModules.fullyFaithfulForget}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  The forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\) from sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to the
  underlying sheaves of abelian groups is fully faithful, hence reflects isomorphisms; the same holds
  for \(\operatorname{toSheaf}\). Consequently a morphism of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism as
  soon as its underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is, and — since a morphism of sheaves
  which is an isomorphism on every member of a basis of the topology is an isomorphism — it suffices
  to check \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) on the basis of distinguished opens \(\{D(f)\}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Two localizations at the same set are canonically isomorphic]
  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}
  \lean{IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(S \subseteq R\) be a submonoid of a commutative ring and let \(\varphi : M \to M'\) and
  \(\psi : M \to M''\) be \(R\)-linear maps that each exhibit their target as the localization of
  \(M\) at \(S\) (i.e.\ \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule} S\, \varphi\) and
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule} S\, \psi\)). Then there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
  \(M' \cong M''\) compatible with \(\varphi, \psi\). In particular the localization lift
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.lift} S\, \varphi\, \psi\) is an isomorphism whenever both
  \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are localizations of \(M\) at \(S\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Sections over a basic open localize the global sections (Route B keystone)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule}
  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover, lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover,
    lem:free_isQuasicoherent, lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib}
  % NOTE: to-build (Route B P1 keystone). The \lean{} pin names the intended declaration;
  % the IsLocalizedModule of the section-restriction map is exactly what the D(f)-component
  % of fromTildeΓ (Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib}) consumes.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Tag 01HV, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (item~(4))
  % and \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback} (proof), Tag 01I8 (read from
  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--702, L1262--1276).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
  % as an $R_f$-module."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The second assertion follows from the fact
  % that $\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))$ and hence
  % $$
  % \psi_* \widetilde N(D(f)) = \widetilde N(D(\psi^\sharp(f))) =
  % N_{\psi^\sharp(f)} = (N_R)_f = \widetilde{N_R}(D(f))
  % $$
  % as desired."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Tag 01HV, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves}~(4) and
  \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback}.}
  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(f \in R\). Then the restriction-of-sections map
  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits
  \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of
  \(f\): one has \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, \rho_f\). This is the
  generalization of Tag 01HV~(4) (\(\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde{M}) = M_f\)) from \(\widetilde{M}\) to an
  arbitrary quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\), and is the single load-bearing input of Route B.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover, lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover,
    lem:free_isQuasicoherent, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}, refine the quasi-coherence cover of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) to a finite standard cover \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(g_j)\) with
  \(\operatorname{span}\{g_1, \ldots, g_n\} = R\), chosen so that on each \(D(g_j)\) the
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is presented as a cokernel of free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
  \(\mathcal{O}^{(J_j)} \to \mathcal{O}^{(I_j)} \to \mathcal{F}|_{D(g_j)} \to 0\) (the local
  presentation carried by the quasi-coherence datum of \(\mathcal{F}\)).

  Fix \(j\) and localize the situation at \(g_j\). For a free \(\mathcal{O}\)-module the
  section-restriction over a distinguished open is the structure-sheaf localization
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_isQuasicoherent}: free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules are
  \(\widetilde{(\text{free})}\), and \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\) is an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of the defining element). The section functor
  \(\Gamma(D(g_j), -)\) is right-exact on the standard cover — its failure to be exact in positive
  degrees is measured by the {\v C}ech complex, which is acyclic for quasi-coherent (in particular
  free) coefficients on a standard cover (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). Hence the cokernel
  presentation is preserved by taking sections and by further localizing at \(f\); comparing the two
  presentations shows that, after localizing at \(g_j\), the section-restriction map \(\rho_f\)
  becomes the localization map of the local module \(\Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of
  \(f\). In other words the localized map
  \((\rho_f)_{g_j} : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_{g_j} \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})_{g_j}\) satisfies
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\,(\rho_f)_{g_j}\).

  Now apply Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover} with
  \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\), \(N = \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\), the map \(\rho_f\), the element
  \(f\), and the spanning family \(g_1, \ldots, g_n\): since each localized map \((\rho_f)_{g_j}\) is
  an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the powers of \(f\) and the \(g_j\) generate the unit
  ideal, the span-cover descent concludes that \(\rho_f\) itself is an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for \(\{1, f, f^2, \ldots\}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence implies the counit is an isomorphism, via section-localization (01I8)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_quasicoherent}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule, lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib, lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Tag 01I8, Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
  % (statement and final step of proof) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
  % L1278--1284, L1380--1386).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
  % be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a
  % quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
  % $\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to
  % the $R$-module $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we obtain an associated
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module map
  % $$
  % \widetilde M \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}.
  % $$
  % By construction this map reduces to the isomorphisms
  % $\varphi_i^{-1}$ on each $D(f_i)$ and hence is an isomorphism."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Tag 01I8, \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}.}
  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma} : \widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is an
  isomorphism. This is the affine structure theorem 01I8, proved here by checking the counit on the
  basis of distinguished opens. It is the unconditional instance that discharges the
  \(\operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) hypothesis of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule, lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib, lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}
  The forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and the distinguished opens \(\{D(f)\}\) form a basis
  of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib}), so it suffices to show
  that the component of \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) over each \(D(f)\) is an isomorphism. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib} that component is the localization lift
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.lift}(\operatorname{powers} f)\) of the section-restriction map
  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) along the structure-sheaf
  localization \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\), the latter being an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of \(f\). By the keystone
  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule}, \(\rho_f\) is itself an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of \(f\); hence both the source and the target of
  the component are localizations of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\), and the lift
  between two such localizations is an isomorphism
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}). Thus
  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) is an isomorphism on every \(D(f)\), and therefore an
  isomorphism. (Equivalently, this exhibits \(\mathcal{F}\) in the essential image of
  \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the criterion of Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib}.)
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[The 01I8 decomposition: quasi-coherence \(\Rightarrow\) counit is iso]
  \label{rem:o1i8_decomposition}

    family (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation}, P2).

    again quasi-coherent on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), hence again globally generated by step (1);

4576	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes,
4577	  \texttt{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent} (and Tag 01HV, exactness of
4578	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)).}
4579	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\). The functor \(M \mapsto \widetilde{M}\) from
4580	  \(R\)-modules to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is exact; in particular it preserves
4581	  kernels: for an \(R\)-module map \(\varphi : M \to N\) the induced
4582	  \(\widetilde{\varphi} : \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{N}\) has
4583	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\),
4584	  and more generally \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits of \(R\)-modules to the
4585	  corresponding limits of associated sheaves. The tilde functor is already known to be
4586	  additive and to preserve colimits; its preservation of finite limits (kernels) is the
4587	  property needed here and is supplied as a dedicated lemma.
4588	\end{lemma}
4589	
4590	% NOTE: The Lean instance AlgebraicGeometry.tildePreservesFiniteLimits
4591	% (PreservesFiniteLimits / PreservesKernels for the tilde functor
4592	% ~ : ModuleCat R ⥤ (Spec R).Modules) is ABSENT from Mathlib and is
4593	% project-to-build (Route-P P3 sub-gap). Mathlib provides the tilde functor
4594	% and its stalk computation but not the packaged finite-limit-preservation
4595	% instance; the informal proof below is the stalkwise-flatness argument the
4596	% eventual prover formalizes.
4597	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let us compute the stalk of $\widetilde M$ at a point
4598	% $x \in \Spec(R)$. Suppose that $x$ corresponds to the prime
4599	% $\mathfrak p \subset R$. By definition of the stalk we see that
4600	% $\widetilde M_x = \colim_{f\in R, f\not\in \mathfrak p} M_f$ ...
4601	% Hence by Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-localization-colimit}
4602	% we conclude that $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$."
4603	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The exactness of the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
4604	% follows from the fact that the functor $M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak p}$
4605	% is exact and the fact that exactness of short exact sequences
4606	% may be checked on stalks, see Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-abelian}."
4607	\begin{proof}
4608	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (item~(7) and
4609	  proof), Tag 01HV.}
4610	  Kernels in the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are computed
4611	  objectwise and sheafified, and isomorphisms of sheaves may be checked on stalks: a
4612	  morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism if and only if it induces an
4613	  isomorphism on every stalk. So it suffices to compare stalks. By the stalk computation
4614	  for the tilde functor (\texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} item~(7)) the stalk of
4615	  \(\widetilde{M}\) at a point \(x \in \operatorname{Spec} R\) corresponding to the prime
4616	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) is the localisation
4617	  \(\widetilde{M}_x = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), naturally in \(M\): the stalk functor at \(x\)
4618	  applied to \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is the localisation functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\).
4619	
4620	  Now let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-module map with kernel
4621	  \(K = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)\), giving the exact sequence
4622	  \(0 \to K \to M \xrightarrow{\varphi} N\). Localisation \(R \to R_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is
4623	  flat, hence the functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is exact and in particular
4624	  preserves kernels: \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}} :
4625	  M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}})\). Translating through the stalk identification,
4626	  the natural comparison map
4627	  \(\widetilde{K} \to \operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi})\) induces, at every point
4628	  \(x \leftrightarrow \mathfrak{p}\), the isomorphism \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} \xrightarrow{\sim}
4629	  \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}})\) (using that the stalk functor, being a
4630	  filtered colimit, is itself exact and commutes with forming the kernel). Being a
4631	  stalkwise isomorphism, the comparison map is an isomorphism of sheaves, so
4632	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\).
4633	  The same stalkwise-flatness argument applied to a finite diagram of \(R\)-modules shows
4634	  that \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits to finite limits; equivalently
4635	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left exact, i.e.\ preserves finite limits (kernels).
4636	
4637	  \emph{Mechanization remarks (the three sub-steps the formalization rests on).}
4638	  \begin{itemize}
4639	    \item \textbf{(A) The stalk comparison is \(R_{\mathfrak{p}}\)-linear, not merely
4640	      additive.} The stalk of \(\widetilde{M}\) at \(x\) is computed as a stalk of the
4641	      underlying sheaf of abelian groups, so the induced stalk map is \emph{a priori}
4642	      only a map of abelian groups. To know it is the localised \(R\)-module map
4643	      \(M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}}\) — and hence injective/exact by the
4644	      flatness of localisation — one identifies it on germs with the linear localisation
4645	      map: the germ \(\operatorname{germ}_x\) is \(R_{\mathfrak{p}}\)-linear (the linear
4646	      germ \(\operatorname{germ}_\ell\)), and the stalk action of the abelian-group stalk
4647	      functor on \(\widetilde{\varphi}\) agrees with \(\varphi\) on the image of the
4648	      structure map \(\widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}_x\). This germ-naturality identity
4649	      is the load-bearing step.
4650	      % NOTE: realized by the helper `tilde_stalkFunctor_map_toStalk` (the germ-naturality
4651	      % transport, axiom-clean iter-034) together with the public linear germ
4652	      % `StructureSheaf.germₗ` and the Γ-linearity of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app`; this packages
4653	      % the abelian-group stalk map `σ_x` as the R-linear localisation map and identifies it
4654	      % with `IsLocalizedModule.map …`, whose injectivity is the already-built
4655	      % `tilde_toStalk_map_injective`.
4656	    \item \textbf{(B) Stalkwise isomorphism \(\Rightarrow\) isomorphism of sheaves.} A
4657	      morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is an isomorphism on every
4658	      stalk is an isomorphism; equivalently the family of stalk functors jointly reflects
4659	      isomorphisms (and jointly reflects the finite limits being compared). One checks the
4660	      kernel-comparison map is a stalk-wise isomorphism by (A), then concludes it is an
4661	      isomorphism of sheaves of modules.
4662	      % NOTE: in Lean, reduce to the underlying abelian-presheaf comparison and use the
4663	      % jointly-reflecting stalk family on a sheaf (e.g. `JointlyReflectIsomorphisms` /
4664	      % `Sheaf.forget ⋙ stalkFunctor x` preserving finite limits), via
4665	      % `Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf` (faithful, `ReflectsIsomorphisms`, `PreservesLimits`);
4666	      % the ModuleCat-valued stalk path is DEAD (Mathlib privacy of `toStalkₗ'`/`stalkIsoₗ`).
4667	    \item \textbf{(C) Reduction to the underlying abelian-presheaf composite.} The
4668	      finite-limit-preservation question for \(\widetilde{(-)}\) into sheaves of modules is
4669	      equivalent to the same question for the composite of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) with the
4670	      forgetful functor to the underlying presheaf of abelian groups, where the
4671	      sections-level localisation flatness from (A)–(B) applies directly. This reduction
4672	      lets the proof work entirely at the abelian-presheaf/stalk level.
4673	      % NOTE: realized by the helper `tildePreservesFiniteLimits_of_toPresheaf` (axiom-clean
4674	      % iter-034) via `preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves`: it reduces
4675	      % `PreservesFiniteLimits (~)` to `PreservesFiniteLimits (~ ⋙ Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf)`,
4676	      % and the named target assembles from that composite once (A)+(B) are in place.
4677	  \end{itemize}
4678	\end{proof}

163	/-- **Germ–scalar compatibility (`R`-linearity of the germ map).**  For `s` a section of `M^~`
164	over `U` and `r : R`, the germ at `x` of `(algebraMap R Γ(O_X,U) r) • s` equals `r •` the germ of
165	`s` (the `R`-action on the stalk being the localisation one through `tilde.toStalk R x`).  This is
166	the section-level half of "the `Ab`-stalk map of `~f` is `R`-linear".  Project-local because the
167	linear germ `StructureSheaf.germₗ` is not exported (no `public`); it is rebuilt here from the public
168	`PresheafOfModules.germ_smul` and `StructureSheaf.algebraMap_germ_apply`. -/
169	theorem tilde_germ_algebraMap_smul {M : ModuleCat R} (U : (Spec (.of R)).Opens)
170	    (x : PrimeSpectrum.Top R) (hxU : x ∈ U) (r : R) (s : Γ(AlgebraicGeometry.tilde M, U)) :
171	    (ConcreteCategory.hom
172	        ((AlgebraicGeometry.tilde M).presheaf.germ U x hxU))
173	        ((algebraMap R Γ(Spec (.of R), U) r) • s)
174	      = r • (ConcreteCategory.hom
175	        ((AlgebraicGeometry.tilde M).presheaf.germ U x hxU)) s := by
176	  erw [PresheafOfModules.germ_smul, StructureSheaf.algebraMap_germ_apply]
177	  rfl
178	
179	/-- **Sub-step (A): the `Ab`-stalk map `σ_x` is `R`-linear.**  The stalk-functor image
180	`σ_x := (stalkFunctor Ab x).map (toPresheaf.map (~f))` is a priori only an `Ab`-morphism between
181	the stalks; this packages it as the genuine `R`-linear map `M_𝔭 →ₗ[R] N_𝔭`.  Project-local: the
182	`ModuleCat R`-valued stalk functor for `SheafOfModules` is not exported (its building blocks
183	`stalkIsoₗ`/`toStalkₗ'` are private), so the linear structure is reconstructed here on the public
184	`Ab` stalk via germ-linearity. -/
185	noncomputable def stalkMapₗ {M N : ModuleCat R} (f : M ⟶ N) (x : PrimeSpectrum.Top R) :
186	    (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde M).presheaf.stalk x →ₗ[R]
187	      (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde N).presheaf.stalk x where
188	  toFun := (TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor _ x).map
189	    ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf (Spec (.of R))).map (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.map f))
190	  map_add' a b := map_add _ a b
191	  map_smul' r ζ := by
192	    dsimp only [RingHom.id_apply]
193	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, s, rfl⟩ := TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde M).presheaf x ζ
194	    rw [← tilde_germ_algebraMap_smul U x hxU r s]
195	    erw [TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_germ_apply U x hxU
196	        ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf (Spec (.of R))).map (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.map f)),
197	      TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_germ_apply U x hxU
198	        ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf (Spec (.of R))).map (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.map f))]
199	    rw [Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf_map, Scheme.Modules.mapPresheaf_app]
200	    simp only [Opposite.unop_op]
201	    erw [Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul, tilde_germ_algebraMap_smul U x hxU r]
202	    rfl
203	
204	/-- **Identification of `σ_x` with the localised module map.**  The `R`-linear `Ab`-stalk map
205	`stalkMapₗ f x` is exactly the localisation `M_𝔭 →ₗ[R] N_𝔭` of `f`, i.e.
206	`IsLocalizedModule.map _ (toStalk M x) (toStalk N x) f`.  Both are `R`-linear and agree on the image
207	of the localisation map `tilde.toStalk M x` (by `tilde_stalkFunctor_map_toStalk`), so they coincide
208	by `IsLocalizedModule.ext`.  Project-local: it packages the otherwise opaque germ-induced stalk map
209	as the concrete localised map, whose flatness-injectivity is `tilde_toStalk_map_injective`. -/
210	theorem stalkMapₗ_eq {M N : ModuleCat R} (f : M ⟶ N) (x : PrimeSpectrum.Top R) :
211	    stalkMapₗ f x = IsLocalizedModule.map x.asIdeal.primeCompl
212	      (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.toStalk M x).hom (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.toStalk N x).hom
213	      f.hom := by
214	  apply IsLocalizedModule.ext x.asIdeal.primeCompl (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.toStalk M x).hom
215	    (fun s => IsLocalizedModule.map_units (AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.toStalk N x).hom s)
216	  ext m
217	  change stalkMapₗ f x ((AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.toStalk M x).hom m) = _
218	  rw [LinearMap.comp_apply, IsLocalizedModule.map_apply]
219	  exact tilde_stalkFunctor_map_toStalk f x m
220	
221	/-- **Stalkwise injectivity of `~f` for a monomorphism `f`.**  For an injective `R`-module map
222	`f`, the `R`-linear `Ab`-stalk map `σ_x = stalkMapₗ f x` of `~f` is injective at every point `x`.
223	This is the stalkwise-flatness contribution to mono-preservation of `~`, now stated on the genuine
224	linear stalk map: it combines the identification `stalkMapₗ_eq` with the localisation injectivity
225	`tilde_toStalk_map_injective`.  Project-local stepping stone toward `tildePreservesFiniteLimits`. -/
226	theorem stalkMapₗ_injective {M N : ModuleCat R} (f : M ⟶ N) (hf : Function.Injective f.hom)
227	    (x : PrimeSpectrum.Top R) : Function.Injective (stalkMapₗ f x) := by
228	  rw [stalkMapₗ_eq]
229	  exact tilde_toStalk_map_injective f hf x
